findings.message: N+1 query pattern: the handler first fetches all item ids in one query, then issues a separate `session.get(Item, iid)` per id inside the loop. For N items this performs N+1 round-trips to the database, which scales linearly with table size on an unbounded endpoint and will exhaust the connection pool / blow request latency well past any reasonable SLO as the items table grows.
findings.suggestion: Fetch the names in a single query, e.g. `names = list(session.scalars(select(Item.name).order_by(Item.id)))`. That replaces N+1 round-trips with one and removes the need for the per-row `session.get` lookups (and the `# type: ignore[union-attr]`).

findings.message: Unbounded result set: `/items/names` returns every row in the `items` table with no pagination/limit. Combined with the N+1 above, a moderately sized table turns a single request into thousands of queries and a large JSON payload, risking pool exhaustion and slow responses under concurrent load.
findings.suggestion: Add pagination (e.g. `limit`/`offset` query params with a sane default and hard cap) and apply it in the SQL query itself.

findings.message: Allocation pattern in the hot path: building `names` via repeated `list.append` inside the per-row loop is fine functionally, but together with the per-row `session.get` it materialises N ORM instances into the identity map just to read a single string column. Once the N+1 is fixed by selecting `Item.name` directly, no ORM instances need to be allocated at all.
findings.suggestion: After collapsing to a single `select(Item.name)`, drop the intermediate list-and-append entirely and return the `session.scalars(...).all()` (or `list(...)`) result directly.
